name: CEOR
description: CEOR is a modern method that begins by permuting corners and building a first block simultaneously (CPFB), followed by edge orientation with the dM slice, then blockbuilding to complete the first two layers, and finishing with a reduced last layer. By solving corner permutation early, CEOR reduces the last layer to a smaller algorithm set. It represents the growing trend of CP-first methods in modern speedcubing theory.

steps.name: CPFB (Corner Permutation + First Block)
steps.description: Simultaneously permute all corners into their correct orbits and build a 1x2x3 first block on the left side. Corner permutation is solved relative to the first block, ensuring that CP is resolved for the rest of the solve.
tips: This is the hardest step to plan in inspection
Learn to recognize corner permutation states
Practice combining CP resolution with block building

steps.name: EOdM (Edge Orientation + dM Slice)
steps.description: Orient all remaining edges while placing edges into the dM slice (the down and middle layer columns on the right side). This step prepares the cube for efficient right-block construction.
tips: dM placement reduces work in the next step
Use R and U moves primarily

steps.name: Right Block (Blockbuilding)
steps.description: Complete the right-side 1x2x3 block to finish the first two layers. Since edges are oriented and corner permutation is solved, this step is straightforward blockbuilding.
tips: Oriented edges make pair insertion efficient

steps.name: Reduced Last Layer
steps.description: Solve the last layer with a reduced algorithm set. Since corner permutation and edge orientation are already solved, the last layer only requires corner orientation and edge permutation, significantly fewer cases than full OLL+PLL.
tips: CP already solved means only 2GLL or COLL+EPLL needed
Far fewer cases than standard OLL+PLL
Recognition is simplified with pre-solved CP

pros: Reduced last layer due to early CP solving
No cube rotations after first block
cons: Steep learning curve for the CP-first concept
